origin: A short Greek-scriptural genealogy, likely from the Septuagint (LXX), describing the line of kings: Uzziah/Jotham/Ahaz/Hezekiah. It reads like a neutral historical/chronological note typical of biblical narrative or king-lists in Chronicles/Kings.
author: Not an individual human author in the modern sense; the text is attributed to the biblical/Deuteronomistic historical tradition, transmitted in Greek via the Septuagint translators/scribal tradition. Closest “author” to infer: the anonymous Septuagint translator/editor or the underlying biblical chronicler.
thoughts: The authorial voice is matter-of-fact and genealogical—focused less on persuasion and more on establishing legitimacy and continuity (who begot whom), supporting a chronological framework for the reigns of Judah’s kings. The motivation is to anchor later events in a dynastic line rather than to interpret events emotionally or theologically in this snippet.
audience: Ancient readers/listeners of scripture (Jewish diaspora audiences using Greek) and later congregations/scholars using the LXX—people interested in the kingdom’s history, royal legitimacy, and scriptural continuity.
written_when: The underlying biblical material likely reflects composition/redaction during the Persian/early Hellenistic period (roughly 5th–4th century BCE), while this specific Greek form corresponds to the Septuagint’s translation/editorial work, generally dated to about the 3rd–2nd century BCE (with manuscript copying continuing for centuries after).
